skill.workflow_scope: Triage blocker incidents for the on-call rotation, keep owner clarity explicit, and produce the daily summary that feeds the response review stand-up before any general backlog cleanup or broad queue sweep.

toml.workflow_scope: Triage blocker incidents for the on-call rotation, keep owner clarity explicit, and produce the daily summary that feeds the response review stand-up before any general backlog cleanup or broad queue sweep.

automation.task_prompt: Run `make codex-daily-triage` to triage blocker incidents for the on-call rotation, preserve clear ownership in the daily summary, and feed the response review stand-up before any general backlog cleanup. Keep cadence and cron details outside the task body.

decisions.rationale: The skill anchors on `make codex-daily-triage` because the ops doc names it as the live Codex-facing entrypoint, while the release context narrows the workflow to blocker-first on-call triage that feeds the stand-up rather than a broad queue sweep.

The TOML keeps the same live make entrypoint and must-read set so the rendered config stays aligned with the active workflow docs and the April objective shift, instead of reviving the deprecated helper or stale daily sweep language.

The automation mapping keeps task semantics separate from schedule semantics: the task calls the same live make target, while the weekday 09:00 cadence stays sourced from the cron file and ops doc. The release notes also rule out weekend sweep behavior.

The note explicitly records why stale candidates were rejected: legacy docs, legacy skill text, and abandoned drafts all point at the rollback-only helper, while migration notes state that the live workflow remained on the Make target throughout the codex-native migration.

rejected_candidates.reason: The file marks itself as an abandoned draft, points at the deprecated helper, and carries the stale every-day cron instead of the live weekday schedule. Migration notes say the live workflow stayed on the Make target.
This skill is labeled as a legacy draft that predates the current migration and exists only for comparison, so it is evidence of the old wording rather than the live Codex workflow.
The patch was never shipped and explicitly says it should not be treated as proof of the live workflow, so it cannot override the current make-based entrypoint and blocker-first release context.
